title: Coexistence of d-Wave Altermagnetism and Topological States in Janus FeSeX (X = S, Te) Monolayers
authors: Alvaro Gonzalez-Garcia, William Lopez-Perez, Paola Pacheco, Luz Ramirez-Montes, Rafael Gonzalez-Hernandez
abstract: The interplay between unconventional magnetism and band topology in two-dimensional materials has emerged as an important theme in condensed matter physics. Here, we present first-principles calculations that reveal the coexistence of d-wave altermagnetism and topological behavior in Janus FeSeX (X = S, Te) monolayers. The chemical asymmetry of the Janus structure breaks both out-of-plane mirror and inversion symmetries, leading to anisotropic exchange interactions and momentum-dependent spin splittings even in the absence of spin-orbit coupling, the defining signature of altermagnetism. Phonon dispersion analyses confirm the dynamical stability of both compounds, while strain-dependent calculations demonstrate that the magnitude of the altermagnetic exchange splitting ($Δ_s$) can be efficiently tuned by biaxial strain. When spin-orbit coupling is included, a finite topological band gap emerges at the Fermi level, accompanied by quantized spin Hall conductivity plateaus and nontrivial topological invariants (spin Chern number = 1, Z2=1). These findings establish FeSeS and FeSeTe as promising two-dimensional platforms for realizing topological altermagnetism and spin--orbit--driven charge--spin conversion, thus opening new avenues for low-dissipation spintronic devices.
summary: This paper explores how 'Janus engineering' in FeSe-based monolayers can create a unique state of matter where altermagnetism and topology coexist. By breaking inversion symmetry through chemical asymmetry, the authors demonstrate a system that possesses momentum-dependent spin splitting without needing spin-orbit coupling. When spin-orbit coupling is added, the material becomes a topological insulator with protected edge modes. The ability to tune these properties using biaxial strain makes these materials promising candidates for low-dissipation spintronic devices.
main_problem: The study investigates the coexistence of d-wave altermagnetism and topological states in Janus FeSeX (X = S, Te) monolayers and how chemical asymmetry and strain affect these phases.
main_result: The researchers found that Janus FeSeX monolayers exhibit a stable d-wave altermagnetic phase that, upon the inclusion of spin-orbit coupling, transitions into a topological insulator state with quantized spin Hall conductivity.
method: First-principles calculations using Density Functional Theory (DFT+U) via the VASP code, including phonon dispersion for stability and Wannier-based tight-binding models for topological analysis.
model_or_system: Janus FeSeX (X = S, Te) monolayers, which are 2D structures where one selenium sublayer in pristine FeSe is replaced by sulfur or tellurium to break inversion and mirror symmetries.
key_observables: Altermagnetic exchange splitting (delta_s), spin Chern number, Z2 topological invariant, spin Hall conductivity, and phonon frequencies.
important_parameters: Biaxial strain (compressive and tensile), spin-orbit coupling (SOC) strength, and the chemical identity of the chalcogen (S vs Te).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the dynamical and thermal stability of the monolayers and focuses on the emergence of phases driven by exchange interactions and SOC.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows phonon dispersion for stability; Figure 2 illustrates strain-dependent spin-polarized band structures and spin-density in FeSeS; Figure 3 shows the evolution of exchange splitting under strain in FeSeTe; Figure 4 displays SOC-induced topological properties and edge modes; Figure 5 shows spin-resolved edge modes in finite-size geometries.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of symmetry breaking in Janus structures, presents structural and stability analyses, details the emergence of altermagnetism, explores the transition to a topological state via SOC, and discusses the effects of strain and potential spintronic applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Phonon dispersion relations of a) FeSeS and b) FeS- eTe along the high-symmetry path Γ–X–M–Γ in the Brillouin zone. The phonon branches represent the vibrational modes of the Fe–chalcogen lattice, with acoustic modes emerging from Γ and optical modes extending up to ∼11 THz and ∼9 THz for FeSeS and FeSeTe, respectively. The absence of imaginary frequencies throughout the Brillouin zone confirms the dynamical stability of both compounds.

FIG. 2. (Color online) Strain-tunable altermagnetism in Janus FeSeS. (Top) Spin-polarized band structures for selected in- plane lattice constants a, without SOC. Blue and red denote opposite spin channels; the orange arrow marks the exchange splitting ∆s near the M point. (Middle) Total energy vs a and structural schematic (Fe: brown, Se: green, S: yellow); the equilibrium a is ∼3.68 ˚A. (Bottom left) Evolution of ∆s with a, showing enhancement under compression and suppression under tension. (Bottom right) Spin-density isosurfaces for three representatives a values, illustrating the emergence and collapse of the staggered spin density with strain.

FIG. 3. (Color online) Strain-tunable altermagnetism in Janus FeSeTe. (Top) Spin-polarized band structures for representative in-plane lattice constants a, without SOC. (Middle) Total energy vs a. (Bottom) Evolution of the exchange splitting ∆s near M and spin-density isosurfaces (spin-up blue, spin-down red) for compressed, equilibrium, and tensile states. Compressive strain enhances ∆s and stabilizes an staggered spin texture, while tensile strain suppresses the spin splitting and collapses the altermagnetic order.

FIG. 4. (Color online) Spin–orbit–coupled electronic and topological properties of FeSeX (X = S, Te) Janus monolayers. (a)–(b) From left to right: intrinsic spin Hall conductivity (SHC) tensor components σz xy and σz yx as a function of the Fermi level; spin–orbit–coupled band structures projected onto Fe dxz and dyz orbitals; three-dimensional first conduction band dispersion colored by the spin expectation value ⟨Sz⟩; and the corresponding surface (010) band structure along the ¯Γ– ¯ X–¯Γ path. The orbital-resolved bands highlight the dominant contribution of Fe dxz/yz states to the low-energy manifold, which are responsible for the SOC-induced topological gap near the Fermi level. The...

FIG. 5. Spin-polarized edge modes in FeSeX (X = S, Te) Janus square geometries obtained from finite-size calculations. The red and blue colors represent spin-up and spin-down wave function densities localized along the system boundaries. The inset presents the energy spectrum of a 23×23 finite square lattice versus eigenstate index, with edge states emerging within the 2D energy gap (shaded region).
